comments.explanation: Proposition 1 states an argmax problem, but Eq. (4) and the IR loss are written as argmin with negative log terms. The standard derivation of the optimal classifier for the argmax objective yields the stated solution, but the paper's own training uses argmin of the negative of the same objective. This mismatch can confuse readers about which optimization direction is used. Rewrite Proposition 1 in minimization form consistent with Eq. (4): `argmin_{d} { -E_{a~p0}[log d(a)] - E_{a~p1}[log(1-d(a))] }` and note that the optimal solution is the same. Alternatively, keep argmax but add a sentence clarifying that the training in Eq. (4) minimizes the negative of this objective.

comments.title: Algorithm 4 KL divergence direction is mis-specified
comments.quote:
| qφ ← arg minq DKL(p(m|E)||q);  |
|  pr+1(θ) ← qφ(
comments.explanation: The notation DKL(p(m|E)||q) is minimized over q, but the true posterior p(m|E) is unknown and cannot be sampled from. In DbAS (Brookes & Listgarten 2018), the actual training objective is the reverse KL divergence DKL(q||p(m|E)) or equivalently the evidence lower bound (ELBO), which only requires samples from q and evaluations of the oracle. The paper's notation implies a forward KL minimization that is not feasible in practice. Rewrite the line as 'qφ ← arg minq DKL(q || p(m|E))' to match the actual DbAS algorithm.

comments.title: Boltzmann conditional probability lacks proper normalization
comments.quote:
Example B. In a softer version of  $\mathcal{E}$  and  $\mathcal{C}$ , we can define its conditional probability of being true to follow a Boltzmann distribution:

$$
p (\mathcal {E} | \mathbf {m}) = p (\mathbf {m} \in \mathcal {C} | \mathbf {m}) \propto \exp (f (\mathbf {m}) / \tau). \tag {3}
$$
comments.explanation: The statement p(E|m) ∝ exp(f(m)/τ) is mathematically incomplete because p(E|m) must be a well-defined probability (between 0 and 1) for each m, not just proportional to an unnormalized quantity. The proportionality suggests a missing normalizing constant that depends on m, but a conditional probability should be a number, not a proportionality. The intended meaning is likely that the probability is exp(f(m)/τ) / Z, where Z is a normalizing constant that does not depend on m (e.g., Z = ∫ exp(f(m)/τ) dm, but that would make p(E|m) a density, not a probability). Rewrite (3) as p(E|m) = exp(f(m)/τ) / (1 + exp(f(m)/τ)) to ensure 0 ≤ p(E|m) ≤ 1, or clarify that the proportionality is with respect to a normalizing constant that depends on m but is absorbed into the posterior.

comments.title: IPR variant claim contradicts IR description
comments.quote: Another similarity between IPR and IR is that IPR also only has one variant, since the Example B is not applicable for this ratio modeling approach (see Section A.4 in Appendix for more details).
comments.explanation: The text claims that IPR has only one variant because Example B (Boltzmann distribution) is not applicable for ratio modeling. However, Section 3.3 (Modeling a Probability Ratio) already states that 'the choice of E in Example B is not usable because of the unknown normalizing constant for probability p(E|m).' This is a direct contradiction: if Example B is not usable for ratio modeling, then IR should also have only one variant, yet the paper earlier describes IR as having two variants (IR-A and IR-B) in Section 3.3. The paper needs to reconcile this inconsistency — either IR also has only one variant, or there is a different reason why IPR has one variant while IR has two. Remove the quoted sentence and replace with a clear explanation of why IPR has only one variant, distinguishing it from IR.

comments.title: Probabilistic BBO target not fully justified
comments.quote: We show how previous optimization approaches can be “reinvented” in our framework, and further propose new probabilistic black-box optimization algorithms.
comments.explanation: The paper relaxes the BBO goal of finding argmax f(m) to sampling from a posterior p(m|E), but never analyzes how the mode of this posterior relates to the true maximizer of f(m). For the Boltzmann distribution (Example B), the mode may differ from the maximizer when the prior is not uniform or when τ is not infinitesimal. The paper does not discuss this discrepancy or provide guidance on choosing τ or the threshold s. This is a critical gap because the practical utility of the framework depends on whether sampling from p(m|E) yields sequences with high f(m). The authors should compute, for a simple toy example, how the mode of p(m|E) shifts as a function of τ and prior, and discuss the implications for algorithm design.

comments.title: Unsupported claim of applicability to any BBO setting
comments.quote: We point out that our framework could actually be applied to any black-box optimization settings, but in this work we focus on its application to biological sequence design.
comments.explanation: This claim is too broad and unsupported. The framework relies on defining an event E and a posterior p(m|E) that relates to the objective function f(m). For general black-box optimization problems (e.g., optimizing a non-smooth, discontinuous function over a continuous domain), it is not clear how to define E in a way that leads to tractable inference. The paper does not provide examples or a general construction. This overclaim weakens the paper's credibility. Remove the phrase 'any black-box optimization settings' and replace with a more precise statement, such as: 'Our framework can be applied to a broad class of black-box optimization problems where a meaningful probabilistic target can be defined; in this work we focus on biological sequence design.'

comments.title: Noisy oracle formulation lacks formal definition
comments.quote: Notice that in sequence design, the oracle could be either exact or noisy, thus we use the more general  $s \sim f(\mathbf{m})$  formulation rather than  $s = f(\mathbf{m})$ .
comments.explanation: The notation s ~ f(m) is ambiguous: it suggests that s is a random variable whose distribution depends on m, but no distributional family or noise model is specified. Without a concrete noise model (e.g., additive Gaussian noise, Bernoulli corruption), the subsequent probabilistic formulation (e.g., p(E|m) in Examples A and B) cannot be evaluated. The paper should specify the noise model or clarify that the formulation assumes a deterministic oracle and that noisy oracles are handled by an extension not detailed here.

comments.title: Missing formal definition of 'proposal' update
comments.quote: e  $r$ -th round of this multi-round setting, the algorithm would use the proposal  $p_r(\cdot)$  of this round to sample a batch of data  $(\theta / \mathbf{m})$  for simulation / query, and augment the current dataset  $\mathcal{D}$  with the newly obtained batch of data. We use  $n$  to denote the batch size for each round's simulation / query. Afterwards, the algorithm
comments.explanation: The text describes the iterative process but does not specify how the proposal p_{r+1}(·) is updated from the augmented dataset. Without a concrete update rule (e.g., using importance sampling, MCMC, or a neural density estimator), the description is too vague to be a 'recipe' as claimed. The authors should add a sentence or reference indicating the general form of the update, such as 'p_{r+1}(·) is derived from the posterior approximation given D, e.g., via a conditional density estimator or a reweighted prior.'

comments.title: Algorithm 3 KL divergence notation is ambiguous
comments.quote: qφ ← arg minq E* [DKL(p(θ|x)||q)];
comments.explanation: The notation 'E*' is undefined and the expression 'DKL(p(θ|x)||q)' omits the conditional argument x in q. In SNP (Papamakarios & Murray 2016), the training objective is Ex~p(x)[DKL(p(θ|x)||qφ(θ|x))], which is equivalent to maximizing the log-likelihood of θ given x under the simulator's joint distribution. The paper's notation is ambiguous and could mislead readers about the actual training procedure. Rewrite the line as 'qφ ← arg minq E_{x~p(x)}[DKL(p(θ|x) || qφ(θ|x))]' or equivalently 'qφ ← arg maxq Σ_i log qφ(θ_i | x_i)' to clarify the expectation and conditional dependence.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 return statement is inconsistent with posterior sampling
comments.quote: return {m : (m, s) ∈ D}
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 returns the set of all queried sequences rather than a posterior distribution or a single best sequence. This is inconsistent with Algorithm 1, which returns p(θ|xo) = p_{R+1}(θ), a distribution. The paper should clarify that FB-VAE returns the final dataset D, and the user is expected to select the sequence with the highest s from D. Alternatively, the algorithm should return the fitted qφ(m) as the posterior approximation. The current return statement is misleading because it suggests the algorithm outputs a set rather than a distribution or optimized sequence.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about forward modeling ease
comments.quote: Papamakarios et al. (2019) claim that the forward modeling approach may be an easier task than its backward counterpart, as unbiased estimation of the likelihood does not depend on the choice of proposal.
comments.explanation: The paper attributes to Papamakarios et al. (2019) the claim that forward modeling is easier because unbiased likelihood estimation does not depend on the proposal. However, the paper does not verify this claim or provide its own justification. Moreover, the claim conflates 'unbiased estimation of the likelihood' with the difficulty of the overall task. In SNL, the likelihood estimator q_φ(x|θ) is trained on samples from the proposal, and while the estimator itself may be unbiased for the conditional density, the resulting posterior approximation p(θ|x_o) ∝ p(θ) q_φ(x_o|θ) depends on the proposal through the training data distribution. The paper should either provide a citation that explicitly supports this claim or rephrase to avoid overstating the advantage. Rewrite as 'Papamakarios et al. (2019) argue that forward modeling may be easier because the likelihood estimator can be trained without importance weighting, but the resulting posterior still depends on the proposal through the training data.'

comments.title: Missing justification for q̂(m) as posterior
comments.quote: In order to adapt this regressor into the update procedure of the proposal of the next round, we use  $\hat{q} (\mathbf{m})$  to denote the unknown posterior  $p(\mathbf{m}|\mathcal{E})$  with knowledge of  $\hat{f}_{\phi}(\mathbf{m})$  and prior  $p(\mathbf{m})$ . The specific construction of  $\hat{q} (\mathbf{m})$  depends on the choice of  $\mathcal{E}$ . For instance, if we choose Example B in Section 2.2 to be the definition of  $\mathcal{E}$ , then  $\hat{q} (\mathbf{m})$  is the distribution with (unnormized) probability  $p(\mathbf{m})\cdot \exp (\hat{f}_{\phi}(\mathbf{m}) / \tau)$ .
comments.explanation: The paper claims that q̂(m) is the posterior p(m|E) given knowledge of f̂_φ(m) and p(m), but this is only true if f̂_φ(m) is exactly the true function f(m) and the event E is defined as in Example B (Boltzmann distribution). In practice, f̂_φ(m) is a learned approximation, so q̂(m) is not the true posterior but an approximation. The paper does not discuss how the approximation error in f̂_φ affects the quality of q̂(m) as a proposal. Moreover, the construction uses the unnormalized probability p(m)·exp(f̂_φ(m)/τ), which is not a valid distribution until normalized. The paper should clarify that q̂(m) is an approximation to the posterior and discuss the impact of regressor error. Add after the quoted sentence: 'Note that q̂(m) is an approximation to the true posterior p(m|E) because it uses the learned regressor f̂_φ(m) in place of the true function f(m). The quality of this approximation depends on the accuracy of f̂_φ.'

comments.title: Unspecified normalization in q̂(m) construction
comments.quote: then  $\hat{q} (\mathbf{m})$  is the distribution with (unnormized) probability  $p(\mathbf{m})\cdot \exp (\hat{f}_{\phi}(\mathbf{m}) / \tau)$ .
comments.explanation: The paper states that q̂(m) is a distribution with unnormalized probability p(m)·exp(f̂_φ(m)/τ), but does not specify how to normalize it to obtain a valid probability density. In practice, the normalizing constant Z = ∫ p(m)·exp(f̂_φ(m)/τ) dm is typically intractable, and sampling from such an unnormalized density requires MCMC or other approximate methods. The paper should clarify how the normalization is handled, e.g., by using MCMC to sample from the unnormalized density, or by noting that the proposal is defined up to proportionality. Add after the quoted sentence: 'The normalizing constant is typically intractable, so sampling from q̂(m) requires MCMC or other approximate methods.'

comments.title: IR does not specify how D' is constructed from p(m)
comments.quote: construct D' from p(m);
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8, the line 'construct D' from p(m)' does not specify the number of samples, whether they are drawn from the prior p(m) or the current proposal p_r(m), or whether oracle queries are needed. In SNR, D' is constructed by sampling θ' from the current proposal p_r(θ) and simulating x_i from p(x|θ'_i). For IR, if D' is drawn from the prior p(m) rather than the proposal p_r(m), the classifier may not learn the correct ratio r(m) = p(m|E)/p(m) because the distribution of m in D' would not match the distribution of m in D (which is from p_r(m) filtered by E). The paper should clarify the exact procedure for constructing D' and justify why it yields the correct ratio. Add a clear description: specify the number of samples, the distribution they are drawn from (prior or proposal), and whether oracle queries are needed. If D' is drawn from the prior, explain why the classifier still learns the desired ratio despite the mismatch with the proposal distribution used in D.

comments.title: IR algorithm reuses variable name θ in return
comments.quote: pr+1(θ) ∝ rφ(m) · p(m);
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8, the line 'pr+1(θ) ∝ rφ(m) · p(m)' uses θ on the left-hand side but m on the right-hand side. This is a variable name mismatch: the proposal distribution should be over m, not θ. The algorithm is about m (the input to the oracle), not θ (which is used in SNR for parameters). This inconsistency could confuse readers about what the algorithm is updating. Rewrite the line as 'pr+1(m) ∝ rφ(m) · p(m)' to maintain consistent notation throughout the algorithm.

comments.title: IPS-A and IPS-B definitions missing
comments.quote: Like IS, the IPS algorithm also has two different variants with regard to different choices of  $\mathcal{E}$ , we name them to be IPS-A and IPS-B. Two choices differ in the detailed construction of distribution  $\hat{q}(\mathbf{m})$ .
comments.explanation: The text states that IPS-A and IPS-B differ in the construction of q̂(m), but it never defines what q̂(m) is for either variant. Earlier in Section 3.2, q̂(m) was defined for IS as the distribution with unnormalized probability p(m)·exp(f̂_φ(m)/τ) for Example B, but Example A (hard threshold) would give a different q̂. The paper does not specify which choice of E corresponds to IPS-A vs IPS-B, nor does it provide the explicit formula for q̂(m) in each case. This omission makes the algorithm description incomplete. Rewrite the quoted text to include explicit definitions, e.g., 'For IPS-A, q̂(m) ∝ p(m)·1_{f(m) ≥ s} (approximated via f̂_φ), and for IPS-B, q̂(m) ∝ p(m)·exp(f̂_φ(m)/τ).'

comments.title: d_φ introduced without definition
comments.quote: IPR works similarly to IR, in that we also construct  $\hat{q}(\mathbf{m})$ , taking advantage of  $r_{\phi}(\mathbf{m})$  and the prior  $p(\mathbf{m})$  simply via  $\hat{q}(\mathbf{m}) \gets r_{\phi}(\mathbf{m}) \cdot p(\mathbf{m})$ . Note that the usage of two models in IPR is not exactly the same as in IPS:  $q_{\psi}(\mathbf{m})$  is also achieved via minimizing KL divergence with  $\hat{q}(\mathbf{m})$ , but the training of model  $d_{\phi}(\mathbf{m})$  is closer to that in IR rather than the  $\hat{f}_{\phi}(\mathbf{m})$  in IS.
comments.explanation: The text introduces a model d_φ(m) in the IPR description but never defines it. From Algorithm 10, we see that d_φ is trained as a classifier between D (samples satisfying E) and D' (samples from prior), and then r_φ(m) = d_φ(m)/(1-d_φ(m)). However, the main text does not explain this construction — it only mentions r_φ(m). The reader is left to infer the role of d_φ from the algorithm pseudocode. Add a sentence after the quoted text: 'Here d_φ(m) is a classifier trained to distinguish between samples that satisfy E (from D) and samples from the prior p(m) (from D'), and the ratio r_φ(m) is obtained as d_φ(m)/(1-d_φ(m)).'

comments.title: Inconsistent round count for TfBind problems
comments.quote: Figure 1: Top score (y-axis) curves of different methods on 3 TfBind problems (KLF11_R402Q_R1, PBX4_REF_R2 and CRX_E80A_R1) with regard to the number of rounds.
comments.explanation: The caption says 'with regard to the number of rounds' but the x-axis of Figure 1 is labeled 'Number of queries (in thousands)'. The text states each round uses n=100 queries, so 1,000 queries corresponds to 10 rounds. The caption should explicitly state the x-axis unit (queries) or convert it to rounds for consistency. Rewrite the caption as '...with regard to the number of queries (in thousands)' or add a note that 1,000 queries = 10 rounds.

comments.title: Area under the curve definition is ambiguous
comments.quote: We also use the area under the curve as a scalar metric for sample efficiency to compare the methods being evaluated. Specifically, since the area depends on the choice of x-axis, we simply cumulate the scores of all rounds to calculate the area. The result could be non-positive, since the score can take negative values.
comments.explanation: The description 'simply cumulate the scores of all rounds' is not a standard definition of area under the curve. If the x-axis is rounds (1,2,...,R) and the y-axis is top score at that round, the area under the curve is the sum of scores across rounds only if the x-axis spacing is 1. But the text says 'the area depends on the choice of x-axis' — this suggests the x-axis could be queries or rounds, and the metric would change. The authors should define the metric precisely: e.g., AUC = Σ_{r=1}^{R} top_score(r) if x-axis is round number, or AUC = Σ_{r=1}^{R} top_score(r) × (queries_in_round) if x-axis is cumulative queries. The current definition is too vague to reproduce.

comments.title: Table 2 caption omits metric description
comments.quote: Table 2: Mean rank of evaluated algorithms with regard to the area under the top score curve for TfBind problems. The rank ranges from 1 to 10. Higher rank is better.
comments.explanation: The caption says 'mean rank' but does not explain how ranks are computed. Are ranks computed per problem (3 TfBind problems) and then averaged? Or per round? The text says 'area under the top score curve' is used, but the x-axis for AUC is not specified (rounds or queries). Without this detail, the reader cannot interpret whether a rank of 8.43 for IS-A means it is consistently best or has high variance. Add a sentence: 'For each of the 3 TfBind problems, we compute the AUC (sum of top scores across rounds) for each method, rank methods from 1 (best) to 10 (worst), and report the mean rank across problems.'

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about composite methods on TfBind
comments.quote: We also find that IS-A and IS-B increase top scores slightly faster than other methods, especially on PBX4_REF_R2 and CRX_E80A_R1. This indicates that composite methods' way of using parameterized models to replace computational procedures is not the optimal solution for small-scale tasks.
comments.explanation: The text claims that composite methods (IPS-A, IPS-B, IPR) are not optimal for small-scale tasks, but the preceding paragraph states that 'our proposed methods perform consistently better than the backward modeling methods like DbAS and FB-VAE' and that 'the evolution method is the strongest one, and it beats IR on some of the tasks.' The composite methods are among 'our proposed methods,' so the claim that they are not optimal contradicts the earlier statement that they perform better than DbAS and FB-VAE. The authors should clarify whether composite methods are considered part of 'our proposed methods' and, if so, reconcile the conflicting assessments. Rewrite the passage to explicitly state that on small-scale tasks, the simpler IS methods outperform the composite methods, and that this is expected because the extra approximation is unnecessary when the search space is small.

comments.title: Contradiction on backward modeling superiority for long sequences
comments.quote: 20. We demonstrate the Fluo results in Figure 2 (right), where we can see both composite methods and DbAS achieve much better results than other approaches. This might signify that for long sequence design tasks, backward modeling is superior to other modeling methods, which is not consistent with LFI *(Papamakarios et al., 2019)*.
comments.explanation: The text states that backward modeling (DbAS) is superior for long sequences, but the composite methods (IPS-A, IPS-B, IPR) are not backward modeling methods — they are forward or ratio-based methods. The claim that backward modeling is superior is contradicted by the fact that composite methods (which are not backward) also perform well. The reference to LFI is also confusing: Papamakarios et al. (2019) is about neural posterior estimation, which is backward modeling in the LFI sense, so the statement 'not consistent with LFI' is unclear. The authors should clarify what they mean by 'backward modeling' in this context and explain why the Fluo results are inconsistent with LFI. Remove or rephrase the sentence to avoid the contradiction.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about composite methods on AMP
comments.quote: Figure 2 (middle) clearly shows that the performances of IPS-A and IPS-B dominate the AMP generation task, which demonstrates the effectiveness of our composite strategy.
comments.explanation: The claim that IPS-A and IPS-B 'dominate' the AMP task is too strong given that only one figure is shown and no error bars are provided. The table shows that IPS-A and IPS-B have the highest area under the top-100 curve, but the differences are modest (e.g., IPS-B: -4.39 vs. DbAS: -5.39). Without statistical significance testing or multiple runs, the claim of dominance is unsupported. The authors should soften the language (e.g., 'outperform the other methods on this task') and provide uncertainty estimates.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about 'no free lunch theorem'
comments.quote: While the composite probabilistic methods usually outperform other baseline methods in most sequence design tasks we consider in this work, the key contribution of our paper is not just about the superiority of those composite methods, as different specific tasks might prefer different algorithmic configurations due to no free lunch theorem *(Wolpert & Macready, 1997)*.
comments.explanation: The paper invokes the 'no free lunch theorem' to justify why different tasks might prefer different algorithmic configurations. However, the NFL theorem applies to the average performance over all possible problems, not to specific problem classes or benchmarks. The paper does not demonstrate that the tasks considered are representative of the full space of optimization problems, nor does it provide any analysis linking the theorem to the observed performance variability. This citation is misleading because it suggests a theoretical justification for performance differences that the paper does not establish. Remove the reference to the no free lunch theorem, or replace it with a more precise statement about task-specific algorithm selection based on empirical observations.

comments.title: Overclaiming the contribution of the unified framework
comments.quote: Actually, we would like to attribute the strong performance to the unified probabilistic framework, which enables us to develop a richer algorithm pool, based on which we can design performant algorithms for particular sequence design tasks.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that the strong performance of the proposed methods is due to the unified probabilistic framework. However, the experiments only compare the proposed methods against baselines that are not derived from the framework (e.g., FB-VAE, BO, CMA-ES). The paper does not include an ablation study that isolates the effect of the framework itself — for example, comparing a method designed using the framework against a variant that uses the same algorithmic components but without the unified perspective. Without such an experiment, it is impossible to attribute performance to the framework rather than to specific algorithmic choices (e.g., the use of neural networks, the round-based training, or the particular probabilistic models). The claim is therefore unsupported. Rewrite the sentence to state that the framework provides a systematic way to design algorithms, and that the empirical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the specific algorithms developed within it, without attributing causality to the framework itself.

comments.title: Factual error: 'Nobel Prize wining paradigm'
comments.quote: The Nobel Prize wining paradigm, directed evolution *(Chen and Arnold, 1991)*, which conducts local evolutionary search under human guidance, is one of the popular techniques.
comments.explanation: The text states that directed evolution won the Nobel Prize, but the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for directed evolution was awarded in 2018 to Frances H. Arnold, not in 1991. The 1991 paper by Chen and Arnold is an early work in the field, but the prize itself was awarded later. This is a factual error that should be corrected. Rewrite as: 'The paradigm of directed evolution (Chen and Arnold, 1991), which won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2018, conducts local evolutionary search under human guidance and is one of the popular techniques.'

comments.title: Mischaracterization of prior work on guided evolution
comments.quote: guided evolution *(Yoshikawa et al., 2018; Jensen, 2019; Nigam et al., 2019)* heavily relies on human intuition for designing domain-specific evolutionary operators, which may not always apply to tasks at hand.
comments.explanation: The cited works (Yoshikawa et al. 2018, Jensen 2019, Nigam et al. 2019) are primarily about molecular generation using deep learning (e.g., variational autoencoders, reinforcement learning) and do not rely on 'human intuition for designing domain-specific evolutionary operators' in the way the text implies. For example, Nigam et al. (2019) use a genetic algorithm with a neural network scoring function, but the evolutionary operators (mutation, crossover) are generic, not domain-specific. The text conflates these methods with directed evolution and misrepresents their reliance on human intuition. This is a factual error about the cited works. Rewrite to accurately describe the limitations of these methods, e.g., 'guided evolution methods (Yoshikawa et al., 2018; Jensen, 2019; Nigam et al., 2019) often require careful tuning of evolutionary operators and scoring functions, which may not generalize across tasks.'

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a probabilistic framework to unify likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization for biological sequence design. It maps existing LFI algorithms to the BBO setting and introduces composite variants. While the notational correspondence is clear, the theoretical novelty is limited, and the empirical evaluation lacks standard optimization baselines. The manuscript requires significant revisions to address missing comparisons, resolve contradictions between theory and results, and clarify practical implementation details.

The paper makes a clear effort to bridge simulation-based inference and sequence design through a unified probabilistic lens. The side-by-side presentation of LFI and BBO algorithms effectively highlights structural similarities. However, the claimed unification currently functions more as a taxonomy than a source of new theoretical insights, and the experimental validation does not sufficiently benchmark against standard black-box optimization methods.

Overview issues:

- Overstated Novelty in the Proposed Unification: The paper frames the connection between likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization as a surprising discovery that unifies two distinct fields. In practice, the mapping described in Section 2.2 is a direct notational correspondence: maximizing a score function is mathematically equivalent to minimizing a distance metric or defining a threshold event. The algorithms presented in Section 3 are essentially direct translations of established LFI methods like SMC-ABC, SNP, and SNR into the BBO vocabulary. While translating methods between fields can be useful, the paper does not demonstrate how this unification yields fundamentally new theoretical insights or algorithms that break the existing paradigm. The contribution reads more like a taxonomy than a novel framework. To strengthen this, the authors should explicitly derive a new algorithm or theoretical bound that is only accessible through this unified view, rather than presenting known methods under a new label.

- Incomplete Experimental Baselines for Black-Box Optimization: The empirical evaluation in Section 4 compares the proposed methods against Random, Evolution, FB-VAE, and DbAS. This baseline set is insufficient for a black-box optimization paper. Standard Bayesian Optimization methods using Gaussian Processes or Random Forests with acquisition functions like Expected Improvement are missing, despite being the gold standard for sample-efficient optimization. Furthermore, the literature context mentions P3BO and other modern sequence design methods, yet they are excluded from the comparison. Without these baselines, the claim that the proposed composite methods achieve consistently better results lacks rigor. The authors should add standard BO baselines and recent sequence-specific optimizers to the evaluation. This will clarify whether the performance gains stem from the proposed framework or simply from using more mature surrogate modeling techniques.

- Unaddressed Contradiction Between LFI Theory and BBO Results: Section 3.2 cites prior work to claim that forward modeling is generally easier than backward modeling because likelihood estimation does not depend on the proposal distribution. However, the experimental results on the Fluo benchmark in Section 4.2 show that backward modeling actually outperforms forward modeling for long sequences. The paper acknowledges this discrepancy but dismisses it with a brief remark that it contradicts LFI literature. A genuine unification should investigate why the domain shift occurs. Is it due to the discrete nature of sequences, the non-differentiable oracle, or the specific structure of the score function? The authors need to add an ablation or theoretical discussion explaining this breakdown. Without resolving this, the claimed unification remains superficial and the framework fails to provide actionable guidance for practitioners.

- Lack of Practical Guidance for Key Hyperparameters: The framework introduces a temperature parameter for the Boltzmann formulation of the event in Example B. This parameter controls the sharpness of the target posterior and directly dictates the behavior of algorithms like IS-B and IPS-B. The paper provides no analysis or guidelines for selecting this temperature. In practice, this value drastically affects exploration versus exploitation, and poor choices can lead to posterior collapse or premature convergence. The experimental results do not report sensitivity analyses, nor do they justify why the threshold formulation is often preferred over the Boltzmann variant. Adding a discussion on how to schedule or tune this parameter, or providing empirical evidence of its impact, is necessary to make the framework practically usable.

- Mismatch Between Continuous Density Estimators and Discrete Sequences: The proposed algorithms rely on neural density estimators to model the posterior over biological sequences. Biological sequences are inherently discrete and combinatorial. The paper does not address how continuous latent space models are projected back to valid discrete sequences, nor does it discuss the distributional mismatch that arises during training. This is a well-known challenge in sequence design that often requires specialized techniques like Gumbel-Softmax relaxation or reinforcement learning fine-tuning. By overlooking this, the paper presents an idealized version of the methods that may not translate directly to real-world discrete optimization tasks. The authors should explicitly discuss the discretization strategy and its impact on sample efficiency, or restrict their claims to settings where continuous relaxations are standard.

- Algorithmic Ambiguity and Typos in Composite Methods: The composite algorithms in Section 3.4 are presented as combining forward modeling with a generative proposal. However, the exact training objective and data flow are somewhat opaque. Algorithm 9 states that the algorithm constructs an unnormalized posterior and minimizes KL divergence, but it does not specify how the intractable normalizing constant is handled or estimated. Similarly, Algorithm 8 contains a notation error in the update step, writing the proposal update in terms of the parameter variable instead of the sequence variable. These details matter because composite methods often suffer from compounding approximation errors. The authors need to clarify the approximation scheme for the intractable constants and correct the notation. Providing a clear derivation of how the composite proposal is sampled will help readers reproduce the results.

- Missing worked example on a concrete toy model: The proposed framework maps black-box optimization to posterior modeling, but the paper never computes these quantities for a fully specified, low-dimensional model. Readers cannot easily calibrate their intuitions about how the temperature parameter or thresholding affects the target posterior without a simple parametric example. A worked case would also reveal whether the neural density estimators can actually capture the shifted distributions in early rounds. This simple setup isolates the approximation error from the complexity of biological sequences. To address this, the authors should construct a toy sequence design task over a tiny alphabet (e.g., binary strings of length 4) with a known oracle function. They can then run the proposed algorithms on this toy problem, plot the true versus approximated posteriors, and verify that the framework correctly concentrates mass on high-scoring configurations.

- Underdeveloped guidance for algorithm selection across tasks: The abstract and conclusion repeatedly emphasize that the unified framework provides a recipe for designing algorithms and allows practitioners to tailor methods to specific tasks. The experimental section, however, simply evaluates all proposed variants on every benchmark without analyzing which algorithm excels under which conditions. This leaves the claimed practical benefit unverified. The paper needs to demonstrate how the framework actually guides algorithm selection. The authors should add a cross-task analysis that correlates oracle properties, such as noise level or landscape ruggedness, with the relative performance of backward versus forward modeling approaches. A decision flowchart or empirical rules derived from this analysis would turn the recipe claim into a concrete contribution.

- Lack of verification that baselines are recovered as special cases: The authors state that previous optimization approaches can be reinvented within the proposed probabilistic framework, yet they never formally or empirically verify this equivalence. Without showing that FB-VAE and DbAS emerge as special cases of the general formulation, the unification remains a superficial notational mapping rather than a rigorous generalization. This gap obscures the approximation boundaries and makes it difficult to assess whether the new composite methods offer genuine algorithmic improvements. The authors should derive the update rules for FB-VAE and DbAS directly from the general framework equations and show that they match the original algorithms when specific hyperparameters are fixed. Adding this derivation would solidify the theoretical contribution.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents a clear structural mapping between two fields but lacks the theoretical depth and empirical rigor required for a top-tier venue. The missing optimization baselines and unresolved contradictions between theory and experiments significantly weaken the central claims. Addressing these gaps and clarifying the practical implementation details are necessary before the work can be considered for acceptance.

Revision targets:

- Add standard black-box optimization baselines (e.g., Gaussian Process BO with EI/PI) and recent sequence-specific methods (e.g., P3BO) to the experimental evaluation.

- Provide a theoretical or empirical analysis explaining why backward modeling outperforms forward modeling for long sequences, resolving the contradiction with prior LFI literature.

- Include a sensitivity analysis and practical guidelines for tuning the temperature parameter in the Boltzmann formulation, or justify the preference for the threshold variant.

- Clarify the discretization strategy for continuous density estimators and discuss how distributional mismatch is handled in the sequence design setting.

comments.title: Algorithm 7 & 8: Incorrect proposal update breaks sequential refinement
comments.quote: pr+1(θ) ∝ rφ(θ, x) · p(θ); | pr+1(θ) ∝ rφ(m) · p(m);
comments.explanation: The update rule multiplies the classifier ratio by the fixed prior $p(\cdot)$ instead of the current proposal $p_r(\cdot)$. In sequential neural ratio estimation, the proposal must adapt iteratively: $p_{r+1}(\cdot) \propto r_\phi(\cdot) p_r(\cdot)$. Using the static prior causes the algorithm to jump directly to the posterior (if the classifier is perfect) or fail to propagate the adapted shape across iterations. This breaks the sequential refinement mechanism entirely. Correct the update step to use $p_r(\cdot)$ on the right-hand side.

comments.title: Algorithm 4: Conditional training objective conflicts with unconditional proposal update
comments.quote:
| qφ ← arg minq DKL(p(m|E)||q);  |
|  pr+1(θ) ← qφ
comments.explanation: Algorithm 4 trains $q_\phi$ to approximate the conditional posterior $p(\mathbf{m}|\mathcal{E})$, yet the proposal update draws from an unconditional density $q_\phi(\mathbf{m})$. This breaks the logical flow. If $q_\phi$ is a conditional estimator, the proposal must condition on the observed event (e.g., $q_\phi(\mathbf{m}|\mathcal{E}_{\text{obs}})$) to match Algorithm 3's structure. If the intent is an unconditional proposal, the training objective should minimize $D_{\text{KL}}(p(\mathbf{m}) \| q)$ rather than the conditional KL. Clarify whether the model is conditional or unconditional and align the update step accordingly.

comments.title: Algorithm 6: Assigning an unnormalized density to a proposal distribution
comments.quote: For instance, if we choose Example B in Section 2.2 to be the definition of  $\mathcal{E}$ , then  $\hat{q} (\mathbf{m})$  is the distribution with (unnormized) probability  $p(\mathbf{m})\cdot \exp (\hat{f}_{\phi}(\mathbf{m}) / \tau)$ .
comments.explanation: The text explicitly defines $\hat{q}(\mathbf{m})$ as an unnormalized probability. Algorithm 6 then sets the next proposal directly to this function via `pr+1(m) ← q(m);`. A proposal distribution must integrate to one. Assigning an unnormalized function directly to $p_{r+1}(\mathbf{m})$ is mathematically incorrect and will cause issues in downstream sampling or weighting. The algorithm should either normalize $\hat{q}(\mathbf{m})$ explicitly or use the proportionality symbol $\propto$ to indicate that $p_{r+1}(\mathbf{m})$ is proportional to the unnormalized target.

comments.title: Algorithm 5: Missing MCMC sampling specification despite textual claim
comments.quote: The downside of this forward approach is an additional computational Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) step is needed to sample from the  $r$ -th round posterior / proposal  $p_r(\pmb {\theta})$ .
comments.explanation: The prose explicitly highlights that sampling from the sequentially updated proposal $p_r(\theta)$ requires an MCMC step due to its intractability. However, Algorithm 5 simply writes `sample θi ~ pr(θ);` without detailing the sampling mechanism. Since $p_r(\theta)$ is a complex, non-parametric posterior approximation lacking a closed-form sampling distribution, the pseudocode is incomplete. The algorithm should either explicitly include the MCMC sampling loop, reference a secondary density estimator used for sampling, or clarify that $p_r(\theta)$ is approximated by a tractable distribution.

comments.title: Algorithm 9 & 10: Unspecified KL divergence direction obscures approximation properties
comments.quote: Instead of applying computational MCMC steps here, we train a second parametrized model  $q_{\psi}$  to model  $\hat{q}$  by minimizing the KL divergence between them.
comments.explanation: The text states that the optimization minimizes the KL divergence between $q_\psi$ and $\hat{q}$ but fails to specify the direction. In variational posterior approximation, $D_{\text{KL}}(q_\psi \| \hat{q})$ and $D_{\text{KL}}(\hat{q} \| q_\psi)$ induce fundamentally different optimization surfaces and approximation properties (zero-forcing versus mass-covering). Without specifying the order, readers cannot reproduce the algorithm or understand its theoretical guarantees. Rewrite the KL minimization step to explicitly state the direction, e.g., `qψ ← arg min_ψ D_KL(q_ψ(m) || \hat{q}(m))`.

comments.title: Algorithm 10: Circular dataset construction for classifier training
comments.quote: construct D with m in D satisfying E;
comments.explanation: This line attempts to define a dataset using itself, which is logically circular and typographically flawed. The algorithm clearly intends to filter the accumulated dataset to retain only samples satisfying the event $\mathcal{E}$ for use as positive examples. This distinction is important because the classifier $d_\phi$ must distinguish between these filtered positive samples and the prior samples in $D'$. Add a distinct variable definition `D+ ← {mi ∈ D | mi satisfies E}` after this step to replace the circular phrasing, ensuring the classifier training procedure is well-defined.

comments.title: Algorithm 4: Intractable posterior KL objective lacks a surrogate specification
comments.quote: qφ ← arg minq DKL(p(m|E)||q);
comments.explanation: Minimizing $D_{\text{KL}}(p(\mathbf{m}|\mathcal{E}) \| q)$ requires evaluating the true posterior $p(\mathbf{m}|\mathcal{E})$, which is analytically intractable in simulation-based inference. While Algorithm 3 implicitly relies on a surrogate over $p(\mathbf{x})$, Algorithm 4 provides no mechanism for approximating the intractable KL objective. The authors should specify how the posterior is handled, such as through importance-weighted bounds, contrastive learning objectives, or a tractable surrogate density. Add a surrogate specification after the KL objective in Algorithm 4 to address the intractability of the true posterior.

comments.title: Algorithm 9: Undefined optimization variables and notation in KL step
comments.quote: qψ←arg minqDKL(q(m)||q);
comments.explanation: The algorithm box contains undefined symbols and a misplaced optimization subscript. The text explicitly states that $q_\psi$ models $\hat{q}$ by minimizing the KL divergence, yet the algorithm writes `minq` instead of `min_ψ` and uses `q` instead of $\hat{q}$. This makes the training objective mathematically ambiguous. Rewrite the step as `qψ ← arg min_ψ D_KL(q_ψ(m) || \hat{q}(m))` to correctly specify the optimization variable and the target distribution.

comments.title: Experimental metric: Cumulative score conflates sample efficiency with evaluation budget
comments.quote: We also use the area under the curve as a scalar metric for sample efficiency to compare the methods being evaluated. Specifically, since the area depends on the choice of x-axis, we simply cumulate the scores of all rounds to calculate the area.
comments.explanation: The mathematical justification provided is incorrect. The area under a curve $A = \int y(x) dx$ scales linearly with the units of the x-axis. Summing the scores across rounds is equivalent to approximating the integral with a fixed step size $\Delta x = 1$ round. This does not remove the x-axis dependence; it merely fixes the unit. Because the authors state that "The total number of rounds differs across different tasks," using a raw cumulative sum will artificially inflate the metric for tasks with longer horizons, conflating sample efficiency with the total evaluation budget. To ensure fair comparison, the authors should normalize the cumulative score by the total number of rounds (or total queries) or explicitly verify that all compared benchmarks use an identical number of rounds.

comments.title: Algorithm 6: Omission of temperature parameter $\tau$ in pseudocode
comments.quote: construct q(m) with fφ(·) and p(m);
comments.explanation: The mathematical definition of $\hat{q}(\mathbf{m})$ in the text explicitly includes the temperature parameter $\tau$ in the exponent: $p(\mathbf{m})\cdot \exp (\hat{f}_{\phi}(\mathbf{m}) / \tau)$. Algorithm 6's corresponding step completely omits $\tau$. This omission makes the pseudocode inconsistent with the stated mathematical formulation and removes a critical hyperparameter that controls the sharpness of the proposal update. Rewrite the step as `construct q(m) ∝ p(m) · exp(fφ(m) / τ);` to align with the text.

comments.title: Algorithm 10: Underspecified negative dataset size for classifier training
comments.quote: construct D' from p(m);
comments.explanation: Algorithm 10 samples a negative dataset $D'$ from the prior $p(\mathbf{m})$ but does not specify its cardinality relative to the filtered positive dataset $D$. The stability and calibration of the classifier $d_\phi(\mathbf{m})$, and consequently the estimated ratio $r_\phi(\mathbf{m})$, are highly sensitive to class imbalance. Omitting the sample size leaves a critical hyperparameter undefined, hindering reproducibility. Add an explicit instruction for the size of `D'` (e.g., `|D'| = |D|` or a fixed batch size `n'`) after this step to address the underspecified classifier training procedure.

comments.title: Conclusion: Mischaracterization of the No Free Lunch theorem
comments.quote: due to no free lunch theorem *(Wolpert & Macready, 1997)*.
comments.explanation: The No Free Lunch theorem by Wolpert and Macready (1997) states that when averaged over all possible objective functions, all optimization algorithms perform equally. It does not imply that "different specific tasks might prefer different algorithmic configurations" in practice, nor does it justify the authors' claim about their framework's contribution. The theorem is a result about uniform performance across the space of all possible functions, not a justification for task-specific algorithm selection. Replace the reference with a more appropriate justification, such as "due to the task-dependent nature of search spaces, as highlighted by empirical studies.".

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a unifying probabilistic framework for likelihood-free inference (LFI) and black-box optimization, specifically applied to biological sequence design. It successfully establishes correspondences between LFI methods (ABC, SNP, SNL, SNR) and sequence design algorithms (FB-VAE, DbAS, IS, IR, IPS, IPR). While the theoretical framework is sound and the methodological derivations are largely correct, the paper suffers from significant empirical limitations. The experiments are restricted to in-silico benchmarks using neural network oracles, which do not reflect the true complexity and noise of wet-lab experiments. Furthermore, the paper fails to demonstrate the practical utility of the unified framework, particularly the composite methods (IPS, IPR), on real-world applications or with more realistic evaluation metrics. The results are also inconsistent with established LFI literature, suggesting a potential misunderstanding of the forward modeling approach (SNL) and its computational requirements.

The paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by establishing a formal correspondence between LFI and black-box optimization, offering a novel perspective on algorithm design. The unified framework allows for the systematic derivation of sequence design algorithms from LFI principles. However, the empirical evaluation is its major weakness. The use of neural network oracles for sequence design tasks is a significant limitation, as these oracles are often trained on limited data and may not generalize well to new sequences. The paper does not provide a convincing argument for why the composite methods (IPS, IPR) are superior to established baselines like Evolution or DBAS, especially given the computational overhead of training multiple neural networks. The results contradict findings in the LFI literature regarding the relative performance of forward and backward modeling, which raises questions about the interpretation of the experimental results. The paper would benefit from a more rigorous analysis of the oracle's accuracy and robustness, as well as a demonstration of the methods on real-world biological problems.

Overview issues:

- Inconsistent Empirical Results with Established LFI Literature: The paper claims that forward modeling methods (like IS) are computationally expensive due to MCMC steps (Section 3.2). However, the experimental results (Section 4.2) show that backward modeling methods (like DbAS and FB-VAE) generally underperform forward modeling methods (like IS-A and IS-B) on the UTR, AMP, and Fluo benchmarks. This contradicts the findings in Papamakarios et al. (2019), which state that forward modeling is often easier and more efficient than backward modeling for LFI. The paper's interpretation that 'for long sequence design tasks, backward modeling is superior' is not supported by the LFI literature and suggests a misunderstanding of the forward modeling approach or the experimental setup. The authors should re-evaluate their experimental design and clarify why their results differ so significantly from the established literature.

- Reliance on Neural Network Oracles Undermines Practical Relevance: The primary contribution of the paper is a framework for biological sequence design, but the experiments are conducted entirely on in-silico benchmarks using neural network oracles. These oracles, such as the one trained to predict AMP sequences or the one used for the Fluo benchmark, are trained on limited datasets and are prone to generalization errors. The paper does not analyze the accuracy or robustness of these oracles, nor does it compare the performance of the proposed methods when the oracle is misspecified or noisy. The strong performance of the composite methods (IPS, IPR) could be attributed to the oracle's ability to perfectly or near-perfectly predict the scores of sequences it was trained on, rather than the intrinsic merit of the proposed framework. The authors should include an analysis of the oracle's performance, perhaps by comparing the methods with a 'perfect' oracle or by using oracles with known error rates. This would provide a more rigorous evaluation of the methods' sample efficiency and robustness.

- Lack of Real-World Application or Biological Validation: The paper focuses exclusively on in-silico benchmarks, which are insufficient to demonstrate the practical utility of the proposed framework for biological sequence design. The ultimate goal of sequence design is to generate sequences that perform well in wet-lab experiments, which are the only true oracles. The paper's failure to include any biological validation, even a small-scale one, is a major omission. The authors should at least provide a brief discussion of the challenges involved in translating the in-silico results to wet-lab experiments, such as the need for experimental validation, the potential for off-target effects, and the difficulty of measuring binding affinity. A more substantial contribution would be to apply the proposed methods to a real-world biological problem, such as designing a novel enzyme or a therapeutic peptide, and to validate the results experimentally. This would significantly strengthen the paper's impact and relevance.

- Insufficient Analysis of Composite Methods' Computational Overhead: The paper introduces composite methods (IPS, IPR) that combine elements of forward and backward modeling. However, it does not provide a thorough analysis of the computational overhead associated with these methods. In contrast to the baselines like Evolution or DBAS, which are relatively simple to implement and run, the composite methods require training multiple neural networks (one for the oracle, one for the posterior). The paper's claim that composite methods 'usually outperform other baseline methods' (Section 4.2) is not accompanied by a discussion of the trade-offs involved. For example, how much additional time and computational resources are required to train these networks compared to the gains in performance? The authors should include a computational cost analysis, perhaps by reporting the total training time or the number of FLOPs for each method on each benchmark. This would allow readers to make informed decisions about when to use the composite methods versus the simpler baselines.

- Demonstration of non-vacuity for the proposed composite algorithms: The paper claims that composite methods (IPS, IPR) outperform existing baselines like FB-VAE and DbAS, but it does not provide a concrete example where a specific DGP violates the assumptions of the simpler baselines or where the composite approach is strictly necessary. To demonstrate that the result has bite, the authors should compute the performance of the proposed algorithms on a simple synthetic DGP where the oracle function is highly non-linear or has a complex landscape that makes direct optimization difficult. For instance, they could define a sequence design problem where the score function is a combination of multiple interacting terms (e.g., a sum of periodic functions or a Gaussian process with a specific covariance structure) and show that the composite methods converge faster or achieve higher scores than the baselines. This would provide a clear, controlled example where the unified framework provides a distinct advantage over existing approaches.

- Worked special case for the theoretical mapping: While the paper establishes a conceptual mapping between likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization, it lacks a fully-specified parametric example that allows readers to verify the correspondence and understand the practical implications. The authors should select a standard, well-understood likelihood-free inference problem—such as inferring the parameters of a Gaussian process from summary statistics—and apply the proposed mapping to derive the corresponding sequence design formulation. By explicitly computing the posterior p(θ|x_o) in the LFI setting and the corresponding posterior p(m|E) in the sequence design setting, the authors can show how the algorithmic recipes translate. This concrete example would serve as a crucial pedagogical tool to validate the theoretical framework and help readers grasp how to apply it to their own problems.

- Missing implementation details for the composite methods: The paper proposes several composite algorithms (IPS, IPR) but provides minimal guidance on how to implement them, particularly regarding the construction of the intermediate posterior models q_τ(m). The authors mention that q_τ(m) is constructed using the learned oracle 	ilde{f}_τ(m) and the prior p(m), but they do not specify the exact form of this construction (e.g., is it a Boltzmann distribution with a learned temperature parameter? Is the KL divergence minimization performed with a specific constraint?). Without these details, readers cannot reproduce the experiments or apply the methods to new problems. The authors should provide a clear, step-by-step recipe for implementing the composite methods, including the specific loss functions, network architectures, and optimization procedures used in the experiments.

- Underdeveloped implications for algorithm selection: The introduction promises a 'recipe for constructing various sequence design methods' and claims that the framework facilitates understanding of both fields. However, the paper does not develop the implications of this unification for algorithm selection. Specifically, it does not provide a theoretical or empirical guide for when to use backward modeling versus forward modeling versus composite methods. The authors should discuss the trade-offs between these approaches in terms of sample efficiency, computational cost, and robustness to oracle noise. They could also propose a heuristic or a criterion for selecting the best algorithm based on the characteristics of the problem (e.g., the smoothness of the oracle function, the dimensionality of the search space). This would add significant value to the paper by providing practical guidance to practitioners.

Recommendation: Major Revision. The paper presents a theoretically interesting and well-motivated framework that unifies LFI and black-box optimization. However, the empirical evaluation is insufficient to support the claims made about the superiority of the proposed composite methods. The reliance on neural network oracles undermines the practical relevance of the results, and the inconsistent findings with established LFI literature raise concerns about the experimental design. The paper needs to address these issues through a major revision that includes a more rigorous analysis of the oracle's performance, a demonstration of the methods on real-world biological problems, and a thorough discussion of the computational trade-offs involved.

comments.title: Scope Ambiguity in Abstract
comments.quote: In this work, we propose to unify two seemingly distinct worlds: likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization, under one probabilistic framework.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims a unification of likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization, yet the body text restricts this to biological sequence design. This creates a scope ambiguity: does the framework apply to general black-box optimization problems, or is it specialized to sequence design? If the latter, the abstract should be more precise to avoid overstating the generality of the contribution.

comments.title: Confusing Mapping Definition
comments.quote: T: \Theta \times \mathcal {X} \rightarrow \mathcal {M} \times \mathbb {R} \\ (\boldsymbol {\theta}, \mathbf {x}) \mapsto (\mathbf {m}, s), \quad \text {s . t .} \quad s = - \| \mathbf {x} - \mathbf {x} _ {o} \|
comments.explanation: The mapping $T$ attempts to convert an LFI problem into a black-box optimization task, but the definition is confusing. It uses $	extbf{x}$ from the domain $	extcal{X}$ (the space of observed data) to define the score $s$, yet the score should depend on simulated data generated from a parameter $ oldsymbol{	heta}$. The text later clarifies this by introducing a new variable $	extbf{x}$ for simulation, but this creates a conflict with the formal definition of $T$.

comments.title: Mathematical Inconsistency in Conditional Probability
comments.quote:
$
p (\mathcal {E} | \mathbf {m}) = p (\mathbf {m} \in \mathcal {C} | \mathbf {m}) \propto \exp (f (\mathbf {m}) / \tau).
comments.explanation: Equation (3) defines $p(	extcal{E}|	extbf{m})$ as a conditional probability proportional to $	ext{exp}(f(	extbf{m})/	au)$. However, $	ext{exp}(f(	extbf{m})/	au)$ is an unbounded likelihood function, not a valid probability density. The text refers to this as a 'conditional probability of being true,' which is mathematically inconsistent. The authors should clarify whether this defines a likelihood function or a probability distribution.

comments.title: Algorithm Output Mismatch
comments.quote: return {m : (m, s) ∈ D}
comments.explanation: Algorithm 6 returns the dataset $	extcal{D}$, but the methodology describes IS as a method for identifying the optimal mechanism. Returning the entire history of queries is not a standard inference output; the algorithm should return the current proposal distribution $p_{r+1}(	extbf{m})$ or samples from it, as this represents the updated belief.

comments.title: Missing Target Distribution in IPS Pseudocode
comments.quote: construct q(m) with fφ(·) and p(m);
comments.explanation: The pseudocode for Algorithm 9 instructs the reader to 'construct q(m) with fφ(·) and p(m),' but this is ambiguous. Section 3.2 defines the target posterior as $p(	extbf{m})	ext{exp}(	ext{hat}{f}_{	ext{phi}}(	extbf{m})/	au)$. The pseudocode must explicitly include the exponential and temperature parameters to match the theoretical definition.

comments.title: Missing Target Distribution in IPR Pseudocode
comments.quote: construct q(m) with rφ(m) and p(m);
comments.explanation: Similarly, Algorithm 10 instructs the reader to 'construct q(m) with rφ(m) and p(m),' which is vague. The target distribution for the KL divergence minimization is defined in Section 3.3 as the product $p(	extbf{m}) 	imes r(	extbf{m})$. The pseudocode should reflect this product explicitly.

comments.title: Undefined Event in IPR Pseudocode
comments.quote: construct D with m in D satisfying E;
comments.explanation: Algorithm 10 includes the step 'construct D with m in D satisfying E,' but the event $	extcal{E}$ is not defined in the pseudocode. While Section 2.2 discusses $	extcal{E}$, the algorithm requires a specific definition (e.g., a threshold on the score) to be executable.
